worth_it_explanation: On-target guarantees are one of the few contractual tools a media buyer has against demographic underperformance. When a publisher commits to a specific in-target percentage and misses it, the buyer is owed additional delivery. Whether that claim gets pursued depends entirely on whether the guarantee terms were recorded when the insertion order (IO) was signed and whether the Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) result was reconciled against those terms when the campaign ended. These checks confirm that the guarantee exists in writing, that the end-of-campaign result was measured against it, and that in-target impression counts billed to the advertiser were verified rather than accepted on the publisher's word.
intro: Campaigns bought against an on-target audience guarantee use Comscore Campaign Ratings as the third-party arbiter of whether the publisher delivered the agreed-upon share of in-target impressions. CCR reports the on-target percentage, defined as the share of measured impressions that reached the campaign's intended demographic, and the total in-target impression count derived from it. When a publisher guarantees a minimum on-target percentage and the CCR result falls short, the buyer is entitled to make-good inventory to compensate for the shortfall. These checks audit whether the guarantee was captured before the campaign ran, whether the final CCR result was compared against it, and whether the in-target impression count used to calculate billed or credited delivery matches what CCR actually reported.

audit_checks.title: Guaranteed on-target percentage is not documented before the campaign launches
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A make-good claim requires proof that a guarantee existed. Without the guaranteed on-target percentage recorded in writing before the campaign runs, any shortfall the CCR report shows has no contractual baseline to compare against. The claim cannot be pursued because the benchmark was never established.

educate.base:
When a direct media buy includes a guaranteed on-target percentage, the guarantee is typically stated in the insertion order (IO) or in the proposal that preceded it. It may appear as a minimum in-target percentage for the campaign's primary demographic, as a floor for in-target gross rating points (GRPs), or as a minimum in-target impression count that the publisher commits to deliver. Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) is the standard third-party measurement service used to verify whether the publisher met that commitment.

The guarantee and the CCR result are linked. At the end of the campaign, the buyer compares the CCR-reported on-target percentage against the contracted threshold. If the result falls below the threshold, the buyer documents the shortfall and begins a make-good conversation with the publisher. The publisher may offer additional inventory on the same or a subsequent campaign to compensate for the in-target impressions that were not delivered.

None of this is possible if the guarantee term was never written down. If the IO was signed without a guaranteed on-target percentage, or if the guarantee was agreed verbally and not confirmed in writing, there is no contractual baseline for the comparison. The CCR report may still show underperformance, but the buyer has no documented claim to pursue.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The IO on file does not include a guaranteed on-target percentage or a minimum in-target impression count for the primary demographic
  • A proposal or rate card from the publisher referenced an on-target percentage but that figure was not carried into the signed IO
  • The campaign ran on a platform or publisher known to guarantee on-target delivery, but no guarantee term appears in the campaign documentation
You may want to confirm that any publisher commitment on on-target percentage is written into the IO before the campaign begins, so the CCR result has a documented threshold to compare against.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a direct-sold display or video campaign where the publisher offered a guaranteed in-target percentage to a target demographic, you may want to confirm the guarantee is reflected in the signed IO and not only in the pre-sale proposal. An undocumented guarantee provides no basis for a make-good if CCR shows the campaign fell short.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser buying a direct placement targeted to a professional demographic, any on-target guarantee offered during negotiations should appear in the final IO. You may want to confirm the guarantee term is documented before the campaign launches so the CCR result can be evaluated against a known threshold.

learn_more.source_title: Standard Terms and Conditions for Internet Advertising for Media Buys One Year or Less, Version 3.0

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore Campaign Ratings documentation on how on-target guarantees are structured and verified using CCR reporting is available through Comscore's client portal. Contact your Comscore account representative for the CCR reporting and guarantee reconciliation guide.

investigate.intro: Locate the signed IO for the campaign and confirm whether a guaranteed on-target percentage or minimum in-target impression count is stated.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Retrieve the signed IO for the campaign. Locate the section describing deliverables or performance commitments.
Check whether the IO states a guaranteed on-target percentage for the primary demographic target, a minimum in-target impression count, or both.
If no guarantee appears in the IO, review the publisher's pre-sale proposal or rate card to determine whether an on-target percentage was offered during negotiations.

If the guarantee was in the proposal but not the IO, note the discrepancy. The IO is the binding document; a proposal figure that was not carried into the IO does not constitute a contractual commitment.
Document the guarantee term, or note its absence, before reviewing the CCR result so the comparison has a recorded baseline.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the IO includes a guaranteed on-target percentage or minimum in-target impression count. An IO that states a guarantee passes. An IO that includes no audience guarantee, or a campaign where the guarantee was in the proposal but not the signed IO, is a fail, recorded with the document reviewed, the guarantee term found (or not found), and the source of any verbal or proposal-stage commitment.
evaluate.fallback_message: Review the signed IO and record whether a guaranteed on-target percentage appears above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s direct media buys did not include a documented on-target percentage guarantee in the signed insertion order. Without a contractual threshold, the Comscore Campaign Ratings result has no baseline to compare against, and any in-target shortfall the CCR report shows cannot be used to pursue a make-good from the publisher. {{voice_recommend}} confirming that any on-target or in-target commitment offered during negotiations is written into the signed IO before each campaign launches, so the CCR result can be evaluated against a documented standard and any shortfall can be acted on.

audit_checks.title: End-of-campaign CCR result is not compared against the guaranteed on-target threshold
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A guarantee that is not checked at campaign end effectively does not exist. A shortfall that goes unrecognized is a make-good that goes unpursued, which means the advertiser paid for in-target impressions that were not delivered and received nothing to compensate for them.

educate.base:
On-target guarantees measured by Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) are only useful if someone compares the CCR result to the guarantee at the end of the campaign. The comparison is straightforward: the CCR report shows the on-target percentage for the primary demographic across the full campaign. The IO states the minimum threshold the publisher committed to. If the CCR result is at or above the IO threshold, the guarantee was met. If the CCR result falls below the threshold, the publisher owes a make-good.

The IAB and 4A's Standard Terms and Conditions for Internet Advertising establish a framework for how make-goods are handled: if delivery falls below guaranteed levels and parties cannot agree on a make-good, the buyer may be entitled to a credit equal to the value of the under-delivered portion. Pursuing that credit requires a documented comparison of the CCR result against the IO guarantee.

In practice, post-campaign CCR reports are often reviewed for the on-target percentage without anyone checking that figure against a contractual threshold. The number is noted, the campaign is closed, and the question of whether the publisher met its commitment goes unasked. The guarantee may have been missed by several percentage points, but without the comparison, no one knows.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The post-campaign report or wrap summary includes the CCR on-target percentage but does not note whether the figure met, exceeded, or fell short of the IO guarantee
  • There is no record in the campaign file of a make-good discussion or credit, even though the CCR on-target percentage appears to be below the threshold in the IO
  • The campaign is closed and invoices are paid without a formal reconciliation of the CCR result against the guarantee
You may want to build the guarantee comparison into the standard post-campaign reporting process so it is completed before invoices are finalized.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a seasonal campaign with a publisher-guaranteed in-target percentage, you may want to confirm that the final CCR report was reviewed against the IO threshold before the campaign was closed and invoices approved. A shortfall discovered after payment is harder to pursue than one documented before final billing.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser whose IO includes an on-target floor for a target professional demographic, you may want to confirm that the CCR end-of-campaign result was compared to that floor and any gap was documented before the campaign was fully wrapped.

learn_more.source_title: Standard Terms and Conditions for Internet Advertising for Media Buys One Year or Less, Version 3.0

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore Campaign Ratings end-of-campaign reporting and how to export the final on-target percentage for reconciliation against IO terms are covered in Comscore's client portal documentation. Contact your Comscore account representative for the CCR reporting guide.

investigate.intro: Pull the final CCR report and the signed IO, and confirm whether the end-of-campaign on-target percentage was compared against the guaranteed threshold before the campaign was closed.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Open the final CCR report for the campaign. Locate the overall on-target percentage for the primary demographic target across the full campaign dates.
Retrieve the signed IO and note the guaranteed on-target percentage or minimum in-target impression count stated for that demographic.
Compare the CCR on-target percentage to the IO guarantee. Note whether the result met, exceeded, or fell short of the threshold, and by how many percentage points.
Search the campaign file for any record of a make-good discussion, credit, or additional delivery. If the CCR result was below the guarantee and no such record exists, the shortfall was not acted on.
Note the date the campaign closed and the date invoices were paid to determine whether the reconciliation window has passed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the CCR end-of-campaign on-target percentage and the IO guaranteed threshold. If the CCR result met or exceeded the guarantee, the check passes. If the CCR result fell below the guarantee and a make-good or credit was documented, the check passes with a note. If the CCR result fell below the guarantee and no make-good or credit was pursued, the check is a fail, recorded with the CCR on-target percentage, the IO guarantee, the gap in percentage points, and whether the campaign has been closed and invoiced.
evaluate.fallback_message: Enter the CCR on-target percentage and the IO guaranteed threshold above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the end-of-campaign Comscore Campaign Ratings result for {{site_name}} was not compared against the guaranteed on-target threshold in the insertion order before the campaign was closed. A guarantee that is not checked does not protect the advertiser: any in-target shortfall the CCR report shows goes unrecognized, and no make-good is pursued. {{voice_recommend}} adding an explicit post-campaign step that compares the final CCR on-target percentage against the IO threshold before invoices are approved, and documenting the comparison in the campaign file so any shortfall has a written record to support a make-good or credit discussion with the publisher.

audit_checks.title: In-target impression count billed or credited is not verified against the CCR-measured figure
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An IO guarantee may specify a minimum in-target impression count rather than a percentage. If the count used for billing or credit is taken from the publisher's ad server rather than from CCR, the advertiser may pay for in-target impressions that CCR did not confirm, or receive a credit that understates the shortfall. The discrepancy is only visible when the CCR-derived count is pulled and compared.

educate.base:
When a campaign is bought against a guaranteed in-target impression count, there are two ways that count can be calculated: from the publisher's ad server data or from Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR). The two numbers will not match. The publisher's ad server reports total served impressions and may apply its own demographic targeting data to estimate how many were in-target. CCR reports the count of impressions it actually measured and classified as in-target using its panel-plus-census methodology. The CCR figure is what the guarantee was negotiated against since CCR was named as the measurement standard in the IO.

When billing or make-good credits are calculated using the publisher's in-target estimate rather than the CCR-measured count, the advertiser may pay for in-target delivery that CCR does not confirm. Alternatively, if the publisher under-delivered and a make-good is offered, the credit may be based on a publisher-reported in-target count that overstates what CCR measured, leaving the advertiser with a smaller credit than the CCR data would support.

The check is to confirm that the in-target impression count used for billing or credit is derived from the CCR report, not from the publisher's ad server. CCR reports this as total in-target impressions for the demographic, calculated by multiplying total measured impressions by the on-target percentage. That is the number that should appear in any billing reconciliation or make-good calculation when CCR is the named measurement standard.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The IO specifies a minimum in-target impression count and names Comscore as the measurement standard, but the billing reconciliation references the publisher's ad server impression count rather than a CCR-derived figure
  • A make-good was negotiated and the credit was calculated using the publisher's in-target estimate, with no reference to the CCR on-target percentage or measured impression count
  • The campaign file includes a CCR report but the billing document was prepared separately and does not reference the CCR figures
You may want to confirm that whenever CCR is the named measurement standard in an IO, the CCR-derived in-target count is the figure used in billing and make-good calculations, not the publisher's ad server estimate.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer whose IO specifies a minimum in-target impression count for a demographic target and names Comscore as the measurement standard, you may want to confirm that any invoice or make-good credit references the CCR-measured in-target count rather than the publisher's own targeting data. The two will differ, and the CCR figure is the one the IO was written against.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser negotiating a make-good after an on-target shortfall, you may want to confirm the make-good credit is calculated from the CCR in-target impression count, not from the publisher's ad server. A credit based on the publisher's figure may not fully reflect the gap CCR measured.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore Campaign Ratings documentation on how in-target impression counts are calculated from CCR reports, and how to use those figures in billing reconciliation, is available through Comscore's client portal. Contact your Comscore account representative for the CCR reporting and reconciliation guide.

investigate.intro: Compare the in-target impression count in the billing document or make-good credit to the in-target figure derived from the CCR report.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Open the CCR report for the campaign. Locate the total measured impressions for the campaign and the on-target percentage for the primary demographic.

Calculate the CCR-derived in-target impression count: total measured impressions multiplied by the on-target percentage expressed as a decimal. For example, 4,000,000 measured impressions at a 72% on-target percentage yields 2,880,000 in-target impressions.
Retrieve the billing document or make-good credit calculation for the campaign. Note the in-target impression count used as the basis for the invoice or credit.
Compare the two figures. If the billing document uses the publisher's ad server impression count or the publisher's own in-target estimate, note the difference from the CCR-derived count.

If the IO names Comscore as the measurement standard, document the discrepancy and the source of each figure. This record is the basis for requesting that the billing or credit be recalculated using the CCR figure.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the CCR-derived in-target impression count and the count used in the billing document or make-good credit. If the billing figure matches the CCR-derived count or is within a documented and accepted tolerance, the check passes. If the billing figure is based on the publisher's ad server estimate and differs materially from the CCR-derived count, the check is a fail, recorded with both counts, the difference in impressions, and whether the IO named CCR as the measurement standard.
evaluate.fallback_message: Enter the CCR-derived in-target impression count and the count used in the billing document above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the in-target impression count used for billing or make-good credit on {{site_name}} campaigns was taken from the publisher's ad server rather than derived from the Comscore Campaign Ratings report, even though the insertion order named Comscore as the measurement standard. The publisher's in-target estimate and the CCR-measured count will differ because each uses a different methodology to classify impressions demographically, and the CCR figure is the one the guarantee was negotiated against. {{voice_recommend}} confirming for every campaign where the IO names CCR as the measurement standard that billing and make-good calculations reference the CCR-derived in-target count, and documenting any discrepancy between the CCR figure and the publisher's figure in the campaign file.
